source.document: Town of Sykesville Annual Budget Document FY 2026 (Draft)

meta.data_note: Top-level revenue and expenditure totals are taken verbatim from the official Combined Budget Summary (p.16). Sub-items decompose those totals using figures stated in the budget narrative (pp.10–15). Where a decomposition does not tie exactly to the summary, a small reconciling line is shown and labeled. Every figure is traceable to the source document.

headline.change_reason: Lower number of capital expenditures this cycle; general operating expenses were relatively flat.
headline.property_tax_rate_note: Held at $0.32 since FY2025 — cut 1¢ from $0.33.

notes: The $2.1M drop is almost entirely fewer one-time capital projects — day-to-day operating costs stayed essentially flat.
The property tax rate has held at $0.32 since FY2025, when it was cut a penny from $0.33.
Income tax is budgeted conservatively at $1,244,593 — below the FY2024 actual of $1,292,496 — to cushion against a possible downturn.

items.name: Impact Fees (restricted)
items.note: Growth-related fees; $70,000 used in FY26 for a police vehicle.

categories.name: Property Taxes
categories.blurb: Taxes on the assessed value of land and buildings. The single largest revenue source the Town controls directly.

children.name: Real property tax
children.note: Rate of $0.32 per $100 applied to a $624,481,697 assessable base.

children.name: Other property tax
children.note: Reconciling difference between the p.16 summary total and the stated real-property figure.

categories.name: Intergovernmental
categories.blurb: Money from other governments — the State's income-tax share, police-service compensation, and gas-tax road funding.

children.name: Local income tax
children.note: Town's share of state income tax. Budgeted near the 3-year average; FY24 actual was $1,292,496.

children.name: State police aid (Town–County Agreement)
children.note: Compensation for police services the Town provides that the State/County would otherwise cover.

children.name: Highway User Revenue
children.note: State gas-tax and vehicle-fee money, restricted to roads.

children.name: Other intergovernmental
children.note: Reconciling line to the p.16 summary total.

categories.name: Other Sources
categories.blurb: Largely one-time money: federal pandemic-recovery funds, capital grants, and reserves drawn down for specific projects. This bucket is big in FY26 but most of it does not repeat.

children.name: Federal Recovery Funds (ARPA / SLFRF)
children.note: Must be spent by end of 2026. Funds the Oklahoma Ave/Main Street project. Town received $3,115,422 total.

children.name: Capital grants (reimbursements)
children.note: Strategic Demolition/Community Legacy ($450K), Program Open Space ($150K), State Capital Grant ($200K), Safe Streets 4 All ($160K).

children.name: Fund balance & reserves used
children.note: Capital Reserve ($100K), Impact Fees ($70K), Unassigned Fund ($190K for legal fees, transportation planning, zoning rewrite).

children.name: Interest income & other
children.note: Largest recurring piece of 'Other'; mainly interest on Town investment accounts.

categories.name: Local / Program Revenues
categories.blurb: Revenue from Town programs and local activity.

categories.name: Licenses & Permits
categories.blurb: Fees for building permits, business licenses, and similar approvals.

groups.name: Capital Projects
groups.blurb: One-time investments in long-lived assets — roads, buildings, equipment — much of it grant- and federally funded this year.

groups.name: General Government & Administration
groups.blurb: Running the Town: staff, legal and engineering services, the Mayor and Council, and citizen commissions.

children.name: Administration
children.note: Salaries for six employees (Town Manager, Clerk, Parks & Rec Coordinator, Planner, Admin Assistant, Treasurer) plus Town House utilities.

children.name: Professional Services
children.note: Legal ($200K, largely Warfield litigation), engineering, audit, insurance, and the zoning-ordinance rewrite ($200K). $160K offset by grants.

children.name: Mayor & Town Council
children.note: Includes the annual donation to the Freedom District Fire Department, raised from $10,000 to $20,000.

children.name: Town Commissions
children.note: Planning Commission and Historic District Commission training.

groups.name: Public Safety (Police)
groups.blurb: The Sykesville Police Department: ten sworn officers, a Chief, an administrative assistant, plus vehicles, building, and training.

groups.name: Employee Benefits
groups.blurb: Town-wide benefits across all departments: health insurance (just over half of this), retirement, disability, workers' comp, and payroll taxes.

children.name: Employee Benefits (all departments)
children.note: Health insurance rose 3.5% from FY25.

groups.name: Public Works & Environment
groups.blurb: Trash and recycling, snow removal, street lighting, mowing, and stormwater maintenance.

children.name: Public Works & Sanitation
children.note: 6.5 employees plus the Director; includes County tipping fees and the Parkside garbage rebate.

children.name: Streets & Roads
children.note: Mostly street-lighting utilities and snow-removal costs.

groups.name: Culture, Heritage & Community
groups.blurb: The things that make Sykesville Sykesville: the Post Office & Visitor Center, the Gate House Museum, and historic buildings.

children.name: Post Office & Visitor Center
children.note: Three part-time employees; largest cost is pre-paid postage (reimbursed).

children.name: Gate House Museum
children.note: Full-time curator, part-time assistant, utilities, operations.

children.name: Historic Buildings
children.note: Historic Black Schoolhouse and Sykesville Station upkeep.

children.name: Military Memorial Bricks
children.note: Veterans' memorial bricks at the Town House. The usual $50K Downtown Sykesville Connection line is $0 this year — it was advanced for 10 years.

groups.name: Parks, Recreation & Open Space
groups.blurb: Park upkeep, the splash pad, tree care, and community events like Music in the Park and the Sykesville Cinema series.

children.name: Parks Maintenance
children.note: Open space, splash pad, tree care, landscaping, Little Sykes, NPDES stormwater permit fee.

children.name: Parks & Recreation (events)
children.note: SPARC events: Sykesville Cinema series, Music in the Park.

town_government.how_adopted: The budget is proposed by the Town Manager and adopted by the Mayor and Town Council by ordinance, after public meetings where residents can comment.
town_government.engagement: Town budgets are reviewed and adopted each spring. Check the Town website for the current meeting schedule and how to share your views before the budget is finalized.

facts: The Town carries zero long-term debt.
The property tax rate held steady at $0.32 per $100 after a 1-cent cut in FY2025.
At $0.32, the rate sits just above the Constant Yield Rate of $0.2996 — meaning the Town collects modestly more than 'no real increase'.
The Stabilization (rainy-day) Reserve is fully funded at $1,000,000.
FY2026 spending is down 23% from FY2025, almost entirely because fewer big capital projects are scheduled.
The largest single project — the $1.2M Oklahoma Ave/Main Street improvements — is paid for with federal pandemic-recovery funds that must be spent by the end of 2026.
More than $800,000 of FY2026 capital work is grant-funded, reducing the burden on local taxpayers.
